behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the CLI-to-real-git interaction: rename detection depends on actual git diff/status behavior (including rename heuristics), not just local parsing logic. A mock/stub of git output can hide real misclassification where the tool receives unexpected real status codes/fields. No AI dependency and no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real temp git repo, commit a file, rename it, run the CLI against that repo, and assert the report shows the file only in `renamed` and not in `added`/`deleted`.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Real binary diff handling failures usually occur at real git/process I/O boundaries (encoding, NUL bytes, binary patch markers, subprocess output handling). Mocked text fixtures can miss crash conditions, so mocking can hide the real failure. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: In a real git repo, add/modify a binary file (e.g., random bytes), run the CLI, assert exit success and that binary changes are counted in the appropriate category.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real repository state plus real git command/error semantics for unborn HEAD/no-commit repos. The bug often appears in subprocess exit-code/stderr handling, which mocks can flatten away. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real empty git repo with zero commits, run the CLI, assert successful execution and an empty structured report (not an exception/error output).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly targets agreement with actual repository history, so minimum reliable coverage needs a real repo history and end-to-end CLI/git interaction. Pure unit tests of counting logic can pass while real history traversal/parsing fails. No AI dependency or multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Build a real repo history with known added/deleted/renamed/modified events across commits, run the CLI, and assert category counts exactly match expected history-derived totals.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Staged vs unstaged semantics are owned by real git index/worktree behavior; this is a boundary interaction issue, not just one-function logic. Fakes can miss differences in how real git exposes staged/unstaged sets. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: In a real repo, create equivalent changes once as unstaged and once staged, run the CLI for each state, and assert identical structured output.
